title: File I/O
summary: Read from and write to files using Node.js fs module with TypeScript type definitions

sections.title: File I/O Is Like a Digital Filing Cabinet
sections.content: Reading and writing files is like opening a filing cabinet, taking out a folder, reading the papers inside, or adding new ones. Node.js provides the 'fs' (file system) module for this. In TypeScript, you install '@types/node' to get type definitions for all Node APIs. There are two styles: synchronous (blocking) where your code waits for the file operation to finish, and asynchronous (non-blocking) using callbacks or promises. For most applications, asynchronous is preferred because your program can do other work while waiting for the disk — like answering another customer while the photocopier runs.

sections.title: Reading and Writing a Configuration File
sections.content: Uses fs.promises to read a JSON config file asynchronously and write an updated version

sections.title: Working Directory, Paths, and Cross-Platform Compatibility
sections.content: File paths differ between Windows (backslashes) and Mac/Linux (forward slashes). The 'path' module handles this automatically — 'path.join()' builds correct paths for any OS. The special variable '__dirname' gives the directory of the current file. For user-provided file paths, always validate them to prevent directory traversal attacks. When reading files, specify the encoding ('utf-8') to get a string instead of a raw Buffer. For large files, use streams instead of readFile to avoid loading the entire file into memory at once — like sipping from a firehose through a straw rather than trying to catch the whole stream in a bucket.

sections.title: Forgetting to Handle File Not Found Errors
sections.content: The #1 intermediate mistake is assuming a file exists and not wrapping readFile in try-catch. Wrong approach: 'const data = await readFile("settings.json", "utf-8");' with no error handling — if the file is missing, the entire program crashes. Correct approach: wrap in try-catch and check if the error.code is 'ENOENT' (Error NO ENTry) for a missing file, then either create a default file or show a user-friendly message. Always assume files can be missing, corrupted, or permission-denied. File operations fail more often than you expect.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Build a simple note-taking app that saves notes to files. Write an async function 'saveNote(filename: string, content: string)' that writes to a 'notes/' directory (create it first). Write another async function 'readNote(filename: string)' that reads and prints a note. Handle the case where the note file does not exist by printing 'Note not found' instead of crashing. Use path.join for cross-platform paths.
hints: How do you ensure the notes/ directory exists before writing — what method creates directories?
What error code does Node.js use for 'file not found'?
Why should you use path.join instead of string concatenation for file paths?
